critical_findings.title: Deferring the real Go/IMAP engine leaves the load-bearing contract unvalidated — and the locked constraints forbid the one validation step the SPEC itself prescribes
critical_findings.argument: The engine contract (VendorPort / send-reply-bounce-thread-unsub) is what the entire facade, MCP toolset, metrics, and deliverability loop sit on. The ROADMAP proposes designing it 'contract-first with a native sandbox implementation' and treating the real cold-cli fork as an activation-time concern. But cold-cli's real semantics are async and gnarly: sends succeed at SMTP time while success/failure truly surfaces via IMAP polling minutes-to-hours later; bounces arrive asynchronously; reply detection is a threading/heuristic race; gws needs OAuth refresh; connections drop mid-poll; batches partially fail. A contract designed from vendor docs + imagination will not match this. FAILURE SCENARIO: at activation you wire in the real engine, discover its result/error/timing model doesn't fit the interface, and every layer built on the mock (facade intents, MCP tools, deliverability decisions) needs rework — at the exact moment real money is flowing and you want to go fast. Worse, SPEC §0.1 (NO vendor spend pre-activation) makes the SPEC §11 Phase-0 'manually spike the full pipe' step impossible as written, so under the locked constraints the contract is NEVER validated against reality before go-live. The plan defers the single component the SPEC itself calls 'the real ongoing job' (deliverability/IMAP), inverting the purpose of sandbox-first de-risking.
critical_findings.fix: Add a Phase-0 'engine truth' step that costs $0 and respects no-vendor-spend: run the forked cold-cli against a LOCAL dockerized mail server (Mailpit / GreenMail / docker-mailserver) that speaks real SMTP+IMAP. Send real messages, detect real replies/bounces/unsubs over real IMAP, and RECORD cold-cli's actual data structures and timing into contract-test fixtures. Build the sandbox to REPLAY those fixtures, not to approximate them. This validates the entire engine contract for real, pre-activation, for free — the only things left genuinely needing real accounts are the mailbox vendor's provisioning API (Inboxkit) and Gmail/MS's deliverability judgment, which are correctly activation-gated. Also explicitly reconcile the SPEC §0.1 vs §11 contradiction in A2.5.

critical_findings.title: The Go engine is a second always-on runtime that 'Cloudflare-first / zero new accounts' hides; a 24/7 IMAP poller is a poor fit for serverless and gets discovered at the worst possible time
critical_findings.argument: Workers cannot host cold-cli: no long-lived Go process, no persistent IMAP connections, and the TCP `connect()` API would mean reimplementing IMAP/SMTP in JS (throwing away the reason to fork cold-cli). So the real engine MUST run somewhere else — a Cloudflare Container or an external VM. The ROADMAP calls this 'a real-adapter concern at activation,' but it is a whole second deployment target with its own build pipeline, secrets (IMAP/SMTP/OAuth creds), and Worker-to-engine networking. FAILURE SCENARIO: at activation you suddenly need to stand up a container/VM host, a Go CI/deploy path, secret plumbing, and a Worker-to-engine RPC/queue boundary — none of it designed or tested — and you discover a 24/7 IMAP poller for thousands of mailboxes is an always-on compute workload that fits neither the Workers model nor Cloudflare Containers' active-time billing/lifecycle (Containers are DO-lifecycle-tied and billed for wall-clock active time). The 'zero new accounts' framing that justified Cloudflare-first is false for the one component that most needs a runtime decision.
critical_findings.fix: Decide and spike the engine host NOW, in Phase 0: deploy a hello-world long-lived Go daemon on the candidate host (Cloudflare Containers vs the existing DigitalOcean droplet vs Fly/Hetzner) and confirm it survives days and check the real cost of an always-on poller. Design the Worker-to-engine boundary (Queue or authenticated RPC) up front. Recommended topology: HYBRID — keep Pages+Workers+DO for the public marketing site, the MCP surface, and the control plane (plays to CF strengths + the distribution thesis + zero-account), and run the Go engine + its poller as a boring always-on service on a VM. Do not present the engine host as an activation checkbox; it is core architecture.

critical_findings.title: A happy-path sandbox mock will train the entire product — including its actual value (resumability + deliverability loop) — on a fiction
critical_findings.argument: The product's differentiated value is durable stateful backend behavior: weeks-long resumable provisioning, retry/idempotency, and the deliverability control loop that reacts to failure signals. If the sandbox emits only success (domain buys, DNS verifies instantly, mailboxes provision immediately, no rate limits, no async bounces, no vendor 5xx, no partial batches), then every retry path, every resumability branch, and every deliverability decision is built and 'tested' against conditions that never occur. FAILURE SCENARIO: the whole system looks green in test mode, ships, and the first real vendor 429 / DNS propagation race / async bounce / provisioning failure hits code paths that were never exercised — the deliverability loop mis-decides, jobs wedge, the agent surface returns errors it has no shape for. The sandbox's dishonesty is invisible precisely because it always passes.
critical_findings.fix: Make the sandbox a FAULT-INJECTING, clock-aware SIMULATOR, not a mock: it must emit the real vendor's error taxonomy, rate limits, async bounce arrival (on the scaled clock), provisioning latency + failures, and partial-batch results. Enforce ONE contract-test suite that runs identically against BOTH the sandbox and the real adapters (recorded fixtures from the local-mailserver spike + Inboxkit/Porkbun API docs), so 'swap the real adapter' is provably a no-op rather than a leap of faith. Fault scenarios (5xx, timeout, duplicate, mid-step crash) must be first-class sandbox inputs the test suite drives.

critical_findings.title: Clock-scaling is asserted but not enforced, and the idiomatic saga tool (Cloudflare Workflows) cannot be virtual-clocked
critical_findings.argument: 'Sandbox clock-scaling so weeks-long warmup is testable in minutes' only works if NO code path reads wall-clock time directly. DO alarms fire on wall-clock; warmup ramp logic ('day N: 5/day → 25/day'), the Gmail '7 clean days' re-eligibility rule, and the deliverability loop's time windows all encode real dates. Any stray Date.now() or real setAlarm delay makes sandbox and prod diverge silently. Compounding this: Cloudflare Workflows — the natural, idiomatic tool for a durable multi-step saga (buy → DNS → provision → warm) with per-step retries — uses REAL-TIME step.sleep that cannot be injected with a virtual clock. FAILURE SCENARIO A: a developer schedules something off wall-clock; it passes minute-scale sandbox runs and then misfires by weeks in production. FAILURE SCENARIO B: the team reaches for Workflows because it's the documented saga primitive, and then discovers the warmup ramp's sandbox test would take real weeks to run — so warmup effectively becomes untestable and ships unverified.
critical_findings.fix: Mandate a single injected Clock abstraction across the entire engine + control plane; ban direct wall-clock reads via a lint/architecture rule (grep-able CI gate). Implement the warmup ramp and campaign scheduling on a DO-alarm scheduler DRIVEN BY that clock (virtual in sandbox, real in prod) SPECIFICALLY because Workflows can't be virtual-clocked — document this as the reason Workflows is not used for warmup. Because minute-scale runs hide week-scale bugs (DO eviction/reload across weeks, alarm coalescing, retry storms, drift), add explicit tests for DO-reload-mid-job, alarm-fires-after-eviction, and alarm-handler-retry, plus at least one longer real-timescale soak.

critical_findings.title: D1 is the wrong primitive for a billing/metering ledger, and cross-tenant abuse aggregation (the master-account protection loop) has no home in a per-tenant-DO design
critical_findings.argument: The ROADMAP swaps the SPEC's stated Postgres (§5 row 8, §8) for D1 without reconciling it. D1/SQLite has no decimal type (money-in-float is a correctness footgun), no interactive multi-round-trip transactions (SELECT...FOR UPDATE-style metering under concurrency is awkward), and a single-writer model. Meanwhile SPEC §7 makes cross-tenant aggregate-abuse monitoring existential — it's the ONE residual risk (Google/MS terminating the master reseller account). In a per-tenant-DO world there is no cheap global view: DOs don't support cross-object queries. FAILURE SCENARIO: metering drifts from Stripe due to float rounding or a lost concurrent write, and/or the abuse loop can't see aggregate complaint rates across tenants because that data is scattered across thousands of isolated DOs — so the auto-pause that protects the company's existence never triggers in time.
critical_findings.fix: Put the per-tenant ledger in the TENANT DO's SQLite backend (real transactions, natural isolation, store integer cents — never float), with Stripe as the billing source of truth and idempotent webhook handling routed per tenant. Feed a downstream read-model (D1 or Analytics Engine) via Queues for BOTH cross-tenant reporting AND the abuse-aggregation loop — that read-model is where the master-account-protection monitor lives. If the team prefers to honor the SPEC literally, revert to Postgres on the same VM as the Go engine (which exists anyway per Finding 2), collapsing runtimes. Either way, resolve the SPEC-vs-ROADMAP datastore contradiction in A2.5 rather than leaving both documents authoritative.

critical_findings.title: At-least-once Queues + retried DO alarms on money-spending operations, with only one pending alarm per DO, is an idempotency/correctness trap the sandbox will not surface
critical_findings.argument: Cloudflare Queues deliver at-least-once and DO alarm handlers are retried on throw. Side-effecting provisioning steps (buy domain, create mailbox) therefore can execute twice. FAILURE SCENARIO: a domain-purchase queue message is redelivered, or an alarm handler throws after buying a domain but before recording it, and at activation the tenant is charged twice / gets orphaned resources. The happy-path sandbox never redelivers or crashes mid-step, so this class is invisible until real money is involved. Separately, a tenant DO managing N mailboxes each on its own warmup + campaign schedule has only ONE pending alarm — a naive 'one alarm per mailbox' design silently breaks; you must maintain an earliest-wins priority queue in DO storage and re-derive the next wake on every fire.
critical_findings.fix: Bake an idempotency key into EVERY side-effecting VendorPort operation, with an 'already-done?' check in DO storage before each step and idempotency keys passed to the registrar/vendor APIs. Make the fault-injecting sandbox (Finding 3) simulate duplicate delivery and mid-step crash so idempotency is proven in test mode. Build the in-DO earliest-wins scheduler explicitly, OR adopt one-DO-per-mailbox (simpler scheduling + finer isolation) and accept the DO-count cost — decide this on purpose, don't discover it.

critical_findings.title: Hosting the MCP on Workers is feasible, but only via the Agents SDK McpAgent, and the 'paste one token' model must be verified in the actual target clients — a distribution-critical unknown
critical_findings.argument: Streamable-HTTP MCP with SSE needs long-lived per-session state that a stateless Worker can't hold; this must live in a DO. Cloudflare's Agents SDK McpAgent does exactly this, so the transport is a solved, low-risk problem IF the plan pins that SDK rather than hand-rolling. The real unverified risk is auth: the SPEC's entire wedge is 'one connection token to paste,' but many MCP registries and clients assume an OAuth flow for remote servers. FAILURE SCENARIO: the deployed remote MCP works from curl but Claude Code and/or Codex won't accept a static bearer-token remote-MCP config the way the SPEC assumes, so the 'paste one line' friction-collapse — the core discovery edge — silently doesn't work in exactly the clients the whole thesis targets.
critical_findings.fix: Pin the Agents SDK McpAgent (session state in DO). In Phase C, actually configure the deployed test-mode MCP with a static bearer token in BOTH Claude Code and Codex and screenshot each connecting and calling a tool. If a target client requires OAuth, add the Cloudflare MCP OAuth provider path before claiming the one-token model works. Treat this as a go/no-go verification of the distribution wedge, not a detail.

amendments: Add a Phase-0 'engine truth' step that runs the forked cold-cli against a LOCAL dockerized mail server (Mailpit / GreenMail / docker-mailserver) speaking real SMTP+IMAP — validates the engine contract (send/reply/bounce/thread/unsub, async timing, error taxonomy) at $0 spend before VendorPort is frozen, respecting SPEC §0.1.
Reconcile the SPEC §0.1 (no vendor spend) vs SPEC §11 (Phase-0 real spike) contradiction explicitly in A2.5 — the local-mailserver spike is the constraint-respecting resolution.
Reconcile the SPEC (Postgres, §5/§8) vs ROADMAP (D1) datastore contradiction; pick one authoritatively.

Decide and spike the Go-engine host NOW (Cloudflare Containers vs VM/DO droplet), design the Worker-to-engine boundary, and adopt a HYBRID topology: Pages+Workers+DO for public/MCP/control-plane, VM+Go-daemon for the always-on IMAP engine.
Make the sandbox a FAULT-INJECTING, clock-aware simulator (rate limits, 5xx, timeouts, async bounces, provisioning failures, partial batches), not a happy-path mock.

Adopt ONE contract-test suite executed against BOTH sandbox and real adapters, seeded by fixtures recorded in the local-mailserver spike + vendor API docs, so the real-adapter swap is provably a no-op.

Mandate a single injected Clock abstraction; ban direct wall-clock reads via a CI lint gate; implement warmup ramp + scheduling on a DO-alarm scheduler driven by that clock (NOT Cloudflare Workflows, which can't be virtual-clocked).

Move the per-tenant ledger to the tenant DO's SQLite (integer cents, real transactions) with Stripe as source of truth + idempotent per-tenant webhook handling; feed a Queues-driven D1/Analytics read-model for cross-tenant reporting AND the abuse-aggregation loop.
Bake idempotency keys into every side-effecting VendorPort op; have the sandbox simulate duplicate Queue delivery and mid-step crash so idempotency is exercised in test mode.
Build the in-DO earliest-wins alarm scheduler explicitly (or adopt one-DO-per-mailbox on purpose) given the one-pending-alarm-per-DO constraint.
Add DO-reload-mid-job, alarm-fires-after-eviction, alarm-retry, and one longer real-timescale soak test to the warmup testability plan, because minute-scale runs hide week-scale bugs.
Pin the Agents SDK McpAgent for the MCP; in Phase C verify the static-bearer-token remote-MCP config actually connects in BOTH Claude Code and Codex, with screenshots.
missing_items: No named runtime/host for the long-lived Go IMAP/SMTP engine — Workers cannot run it, and this is deferred to activation instead of decided now.
No cross-tenant abuse-aggregation architecture — the master-reseller-account protection loop (SPEC §7, existential) has no home across per-tenant DOs.
No idempotency strategy for at-least-once Queues and retried DO alarms on money-spending operations.
No injected-clock architecture rule or enforcement mechanism behind the 'clock-scaling' claim.
No contract-test harness spanning sandbox and real adapters to keep the sandbox honest.
No KMS / envelope-encryption scheme specified for per-tenant vendor credentials on Workers.
No verification that the target MCP clients (Claude Code, Codex) accept the static-token remote-MCP model that the whole distribution wedge depends on.
No resolution of the DO one-pending-alarm-per-object constraint against a tenant owning many independently-scheduled mailboxes.
Unresolved SPEC-vs-ROADMAP contradictions: Postgres vs D1, and no-vendor-spend vs Phase-0 real spike.
